text: The surgeon performs a total laparoscopic hysterectomy on a slightly bulky uterus. The primary port is placed supraumbilically, with additional ports on the left and right sides. Corneal structures are coagulated using a bipolar device and cut with a shearer. The mesosalpingial vein region is carefully desiccated before cutting.

The surgeon separates the corneal structures, revealing the round ligament, which is fulgurated and cut. As the round ligament is completely severed, the broad ligament opens up, exposing its anterior and posterior leaves. Additional cautery is applied to the medial side of the corneal structure to address minor bleeding.

The surgeon cleans the laparoscope lens by immersing it in warm saline and wiping it before reinserting it into the abdominal cavity. The video shows anterior bladder adhesions and the margins of the bladder, which are identified after filling it via a Foley's catheter. The surgeon dissects anterior to the uterine artery, below the previous caesarean incision adhesions, using continuous traction on the uterus to clearly identify the planes. The peritoneum is lifted, revealing the space between the bladder and the cervix.

The surgeon identifies the space between the bladder and the uterus, dissecting the bladder from adhesions to the uterus using traction. Loose areolar tissue between the bladder and the anterior cervix is visible. The bladder is lifted, and the area is dissected, encountering small venous oozing. The peritoneum is opened, revealing adhesions between the bladder dome and the anterior uterus surface. Suction is used to clear the area, and pneumoperitoneum pressure is adjusted to control venous bleeding.

The surgeon cuts the adhesions close to the uterus, revealing the fat along the bladder wall. Dissection proceeds below this fat, reaching near the uterine artery on the opposite side. The peritoneum is opened and released, clearly exposing the plane between the bladder and the uterus. Remaining fascia is dissected, staying anterior to the uterine arteries.

The surgeon bares the uterine vessels for fulguration, performing posterior dissection of the peritoneum up to the uterocycle. Loose areolar tissue surrounding the vessels is dissected, exposing the uterine artery and vein for fulguration using a vessel sealing system. The vascular bundle is clamped at three sites, ensuring safety as the ureter and bladder are distant. Bipolar cautery is applied for additional desiccation, followed by cutting perpendicular to the vessel and parallel to the cervical tissue. The descending cervical artery is fulgurated, and paracervical tissue is cut to reach the vaginal level. The posterior peritoneum is released, reaching the posterior fornix. The myoma spiral is removed and the entry point is fulgurated. The spiral is passed from the left upper port to pick up the uterus, starting the hysterectomy on the right side. The round ligament is identified, bipolar cauterized, and cut, opening the broad ligament leaves.

The surgeon uses a bipolar shearer connected to the cautery machine to seal and cut the corneal structures, generating smoke. The anterior and posterior leaves of the broad ligament are dissected up to the utero-sacral ligaments, with small vessels cauterized and cut. The uterine vascular bundle on the right side is exposed by dissecting loose fascial tissue and clamping vessels at a ninety-degree angle. The pedicle is cauterized as it falls away, controlling oozing from the right corneal structures with bipolar forceps. The Babcock forceps from the left lower port fulgurate the bleeding area, revealing the anterior vagina and fascia. The uterine artery is cut, and the pedicle is lateralized, with the uterine vein visible posteriorly.

The surgeon separates the uterosacral ligament and reaches the lateral vaginal fornix. Using gauze on an Allis forceps, the vaginal fornices are elevated. The shearer is used to open the vault, cutting around the cervix and through the cervico-vaginal fascia. The posterior vagina is visibly separated, and the myoma screw is shifted to the right side.

The surgeon addresses bleeding at the myoma screw entry point using bipolar cautery to maintain a clear surgical field. With good traction, the cervix, cervico-vaginal fascia, and bladder are clearly visible. The gauze piece is repositioned towards the left vaginal fornix, and the vagina is opened over the gauze. Once the vaginal opening is visualized, the gauze is withdrawn, and a mop is placed to prevent loss of pneumoperitoneum. A tooth grasper elevates the cervix while the shearer cuts flush with the cervix to preserve vaginal length, completing the hysterectomy.
